sentences.text: I-- I didn't, you know?
And you had said something about what did I do about clothes.
Sometimes I wonder what happened that I stayed the whole time in the same clothes.
I don't know.
But that one time that we were in hiding in the-- with the Czech man, we were in an attic, and there was a bunch of chestnuts.
And to me, this was such a wonderful thing because there was something I could sort of organize, and play with, and do things with.
The Russians came into that part of Poland in February of '44.
And the next thing I know is being in some sort of a house.
And there was-- there was some Russian soldiers.
There was-- one was a Russian-- I found out later he was a general.
But he was very nice-- very nice to my mother and myself.
And I remember hiding under-- there was-- in the center of this room-- I don't know where this was-- there was a tablecloth that went down to the floor.
Hiding under the tablecloth.
Hiding in corners.

Not really speaking because the whole time that I was in hiding in the woods, or in the attic, or in any of the other hiding places, I could never talk because-- especially in the woods, if I spoke or made any sort of noise, not only did I they endanger us, but I would endanger all the other people that were with me.
You know, that time in the woods-- you know, I-- I talked about it, but my two uncles were killed during one of the raids.
And during another raid, my two cousins were killed.
And I don't know how my mother and I escaped, but we did.
So it was just, you know-- they would just come in all the time and kill people off.
My cousins were 13 and 11, boy and a girl.
Chava and Avram.
Well, I could never speak above a whisper.
I mean, now it's very different.
But for the longest time, I just couldn't speak.
And as I said, I have this vivid memory of hiding under the tablecloth, hiding in corners, crawling away somewhere, always speaking in a very low voice.
Even when I came to the United States, if I had to speak in the class, it was always very quiet.
I would find it painful to speak up or to speak in a loud voice.
When I was first married-- and sometimes, people would call on the telephone, try to sell you something.
And I would answer the phone.
They would always say, can I speak to your mother?
[LAUGHTER] They always thought I was, like, 10 years old.
I've gotten over that.
I mean, I still don't shout.
But I've learned to-- to speak in public and I've overcome that.
But for years, it-- it always reminded me of one of those Jerzy Kosinksi books, where he talks about-- I think it was in or was it in one of his other stories?
Where the boy comes out of the war, and he's mute.
And that's how I felt.
I was just-- I may not have been mute, but it took a lot to get a word out of me and for me to be comfortable, you know, with people.
I was always hiding and crawling away somewhere.
We-- my mother had heard that there were a number of people from our village in the next village over.
That was called Zdolbunow.
So we went there.
And at that point, my mother met my stepfather, who came from our same village.
He was-- actually, lived next door, he and his family.
He was not married.
He and his family lived next door and knew my whole family.
So we were in Zdolbunow.
And that's when I went to school.
I went to first grade there.
Could you spell the name of that town?
Oh, I don't know how to spell it all.
I went to school there.
And--
--this boy, Sam, and I were the only two Jewish children in the class.
We were in the first grade.
Now, the only language that I spoke was Polish.
This class, of course, was in Russian.
So-- but after a while, we learned Russian.
And by mid semester, whatever-- it was Christmas-- the teacher stood up-- it was very competitive.
Everything there was you won a prize-- a Stalin Prize this, a Stalin Prize that.
She stood up and she said, you see?
You have two Jewish kids in the class, and they've become the best students.
And we got some sort of a prize.
I think I got a-- some gloves or something or-- and Sam got crayons, whatever it was.
But of course, by the teacher doing that, our life was miserable.
We were constantly being picked on by the other kids.
And I just remember running home every day after school, just trying to beat the kids who were, like, ready to beat me.
And--
I don't know.
But I certainly was not someone who stood up for myself readily.
If I had anything that one of the kids wanted, it was gone.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at--
--the end of the year, I was going to get a Stalin Prize.
And it was, like, amazing how you become so involved in this.
During this period, my family had decided to try to leave Russia because if you were a Polish citizen, you could leave.
And we wanted to leave.
And I was-- I remember being-- and we actually left at the end of the school year.
I was so upset because we left before I got my Stalin Prize.
I did not get my Stalin Prize.
But I remember being in the classroom when the war was actually over.
I was in school.
It was in '45.
What was it?
April--
--or something, right?
I was in school.
And we were in the classroom.
And all of a sudden, there was just bedlam, and screaming, and yelling, and noise.
And everybody just rushed out into the streets.
And there was this huge celebration.
I remember that.
We had one room and-- and-- I guess in some house.
And I guess this other family also lived there, this boy, Sam.
He and his mother lived there.
And whatever Jews were-- sort of survived from the surrounding area came there.
From what I've been told, the village of Mizoch had about-- was predominately Jewish-- had about 5,000 to 7,000 people.
About a half a dozen survived.
Before the war, he worked-- there was a railroad in the town.
And he did something with that.
You know, I guess arranging, scheduling, or things like that.
I don't know.
I don't know what he was doing when we were in Zdolbunow.
I-- I don't remember exactly when.
I just remember saying I can't leave because I haven't gotten my prize.
But we-- school was over.
I know we were already on a school vacation.
And then afterwards, I realized that we probably never were going to get the prize, whatever the prize was.
[LAUGHTER]
But I never got it.
OK.
And then we left for Poland.
In Poland, we lived in the Lodz.
And we wound up living in the same house, again, with Sam-- this boy, Sam-- and his mother, and I don't know who else.
But we were living in that house.
And we went to school for a very short period.
And then Sam got sick with diphtheria and scarlet fever.
So they decided-- he picked it up in school.
So they took us out of school.
And they sent me off to live with some relatives because-- to get out of this house where he was so sick.
And I didn't go to school.
I just hung out with this distant cousin of mine.
Her name is Ann [INAUDIBLE] who's, like, three years older than me.
And I have this vision of the two of us just roaming the streets of Lodz without supervision.
I don't know where the adults were.
I know that I was not allowed to go back to the house where I lived because Sam was so sick.
And they were afraid I would catch it.
But she and I just roamed the streets.
And we lived on sweets.
In Poland, they have these-- like they have in Austria.
They have coffee shops, where they just serve coffee, chocolate, and [POLISH], which is pastries.
[LAUGHS] And that's we ate all the time, was these pastries.
I think everybody always asked everywhere that we went, and there was sort of this underground connections, you know?
Just like my stepfather came to Zdolbunow because he heard a few people from Mizoch were there.
There was always this question of, you know, trying to--
I don't remember talking about it that much.
But I know that we didn't go to school at-- oh, after he got well, I came back to the house and I got sick anyway.
I came down with diphtheria.
And I was very, very sick.
And I think that somehow or other, my parents were able to buy from the American Army penicillin, because it was just not available anywhere else.
But I was just sick for a long time.
But we had a-- a tutor.
And the tutor was teaching us Hebrew.
So I guess somewhere along the line, we must have been thinking in terms of making our way to Palestine.
That's what we were learning, was Hebrew.
It's possible.
I don't remember.
But I remember the Hebrew.
We left Poland.
And then we went to Berlin for a short period.
And then we wound up in Eschwege, which was a DP camp.
We were in the a DP camp in Berlin-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Schlachsenhausen,' something like that.
I don't remember the name.
But then we were in Eschwege.
And in Eschwege, we were for three years.
Like, '46 to 49.
Eschwege.
Yes.
That was sort of-- for me, that was really a happy time, because I was with children and there was a lot of freedom.
The school was set up by [INAUDIBLE] from Israel, you know-- well, at that time, it was called Palestine.
The-- the people from Palestine would come.
They set up the school.
And the whole school was set up in Hebrew, so every subject was in Hebrew.
You know, geography, history-- everything was in Hebrew.
So everybody just started from scratch.
I remember, though, that there was no basic language that all the kids could communicate.
So in order to-- even though we were learning Hebrew, you sort of needed another language to translate into, and I had no language.
I had-- at this point, I spoke Polish and Russian.
So I learned Yiddish.
That's when I learned Yiddish, was in Eschwege, because that was the sort of communal language.
So then I learned Yiddish.
And then I learned Hebrew.
You know, it was just a regular day, where we learned all the different subjects.
But after school, it was very Zionistic, the whole camp.
And most of the people in the camp, or a certain-- most of the people in school that I remember belonged to two parties, the two opposites-- the Hashomer Hatzair and the Betar.
I-- we belonged to the Betar.
And that was the real, you know, right-wing Jabotinsky.
And-- [LAUGHS] I had a uniform-- that we went to all the meetings.
We had marches for every-- every holiday.
When the British executed some of the Irgun people, our-- our Betar kids all walked out of school on a march.
So it was, like, very, very militaristic.
You know, we were being trained to be soldiers.
I mean, there was no two ways about it.
And this was very appealing to me, you know?

I think if you've been a victim for so long and been so scared, to think in terms that you're being trained to be a soldier and you're gonna go and fight for your fatherland was a very, very appealing thing to me.
I remember we were crawling on our bellies.
I mean, I was, like, eight years old-- that I'm crawling with a make-believe gun.
There were also some of the-- because my stepfather was involved in the Betar, sometimes some of these people would come to our house.
And there was smuggling of arms.
I remember hiding-- that there were guns or other things were being hidden in our bathroom, under a tub or something.
So it was very, you know-- very macho, very much marching around and looking forward to going and fighting and being a soldier.
Well, my sister was born in Eschwege, so I think she was, like, more involved with taking care of my sister and, you know-- and the baby and all.
But my stepfather was very much involved.
He was very much involved in the-- in the running of the camp.
And-- and-- and in the Betar, as I said, there were-- on several occasions, some of these people from the Irgun would come to our house, you know?
I remember I had a bicycle, and that we used to-- the kids used to go out to, like-- I guess it was like an abandoned air-- you know, a airfield or whatever, ride our bikes.
I remember having just a lot of freedom.
The camp was surrounded, you know, with barbed wire.
It's sort of ironic.
You're liberated and you're back-- in back of-- in back of the barbed wire.
So there was no sense of not being able to go anywhere.
So my sense is of the kids just having a free hand-- you know, just running around.
We were in school or playing as a group-- very much as a group.
We were very much, you know, into our own groups of running around and doing things.
Did you ever have any contact with the German civilian population during--
Oh, yes.
We used to go to-- once a week or whatever, once-- it was-- I don't know if it was a Saturday or Sunday.
We would go into town to go to the movies.
And there were-- there were always battles of, you know-- they would still call us dirty Jew and all of these kinds of things.
I don't remember, because it's-- it's possible that there was.
If there was, I was probably hiding somewhere.
[LAUGHTER]
So I wouldn't know.
During this period of time at the DP camp, did religion play a strong role in your life?
No.
It was very Zionistic.
It was just-- I just couldn't wait to go to Palestine.
I just couldn't wait.

Well, what happened was that during this period, my mother-- I guess through the Red Cross or some other organization-- found a cousin of hers who was living in the States, someone that she had been very close to.
This cousin of hers had-- her mother had died and my grandmother had raised her.
And when she found her, she said that her whole family was dead and everybody was-- had been killed.
And she has this family.
And I-- then she found there were some other cousins or relatives here.
And she has this family.
And since she has this family, she wants to go to the United States.
I was devastated.
I didn't want to go in the worst way.
I don't think my stepfather wanted to go either.
But I was definitely devastated about coming.
Right.
Sometime either September or October of '49.
[INAUDIBLE]
It was-- you know, for me, I-- my whole sort of being was tied up in being this soldier for Palestine and knowing the language.
I was-- at this point, I was fluent in Hebrew.
And here I would have to go to another country with a different language.
I didn't know what the people were like.
Everybody else that I knew, all the kids I played with and everybody else, was going to Palestine.
I mean, you couldn't go.
But the idea was that eventually, you were gonna get there.
This was very strange, because we were originally supposed to go to Florida.
This cousin that was bringing us, her husband was already established in Florida.
Anyway, we-- our boat went to New Orleans.
And we were gonna go to Florida.
When we got to New Orleans, there was a cable with tickets for the train.
And it said that we had to come to New York, that we were gonna come to New York.
So there we were.
We were in New Orleans.
We were picked up by some Jewish women from the Jewish organized community.
They were very nice.
They-- they took us by car to this place.
My first impression was driving in this car and seeing all of this neon-- I had never seen a neon light before-- and then being given all these nice fruit, and food, and all of that.
And then we went into this train that traveled from New Orleans to New York-- that traveled through the South along the railroad tracks.
Now, what do you see [? over-- ?] [LAUGHTER]
--the railroad tracks?
We saw these shacks, and mostly black people, and just what looked like this poverty.
And I remember thinking, oh my god.
I mean, like, where are we going?
And that was-- that was, like, scary.
Did you express your feelings to your mother--
I don't know.
I don't know.
But we-- I think we were all a little bit nervous.
[LAUGHS]
We were sitting on this train.
And who was the one who organized this?
Who from your family organized the-- your going from New Orleans to New York by train?
I think that there was a second cousin here, and he said that we should come to New York.
But when we came to New York, we actually moved in with Toibka.
That was my mother's cousin.
She was living in Sunnyside.
It was she and her son, who was then a senior in high school.
Her husband was already in Florida.
And she was waiting for her son, Marty, to finish high school, and that she was gonna go to Florida.
So we moved in with her.
So the six of us lived in this two-room apartment in Sunnyside until they left.
Her last name was Goldstein.
And her first name was Toibka.
I don't know how you would spell it.
She died recently at the age of 100.
She was much older than my mother.
That was-- first cousin of hers.
Her son Marty is the second cousin of mine.
Well, what was hard was that I entered sixth grade.
And I was-- actually, I was, like-- because of this, you know, mix-up in the years, I was registered as being born in '38, so I was, like, officially 11, but I was 12.
But I grew and developed early, you know?
And here I was in the sixth grade.
I was this big kid with braids, with earrings, speaking no English.
And I had gone through, like, a war, you know?
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, while the children were trying to be kind, it was very strange for me.
It was very difficult.
The teacher was very, very nice, you know?
She always took extra time out and would sit with me with children's books and teach me English.
And I remember there was a rabbi also who was very nice, and he put me into Hebrew school.
So while everybody was trying to learn Hebrew-- [LAUGHS]
--I was sitting there.
Knowing the Hebrew, learning the English through the Hebrew.
I learned very quickly, because by the following year, when I entered junior high school, I remember thinking-- I was hoping that I would already, you know, sound American.
I could speak, but I couldn't, you know-- I still had the accent.
And I had my braids-- I cut off my braids.
I got rid of the earrings.
But everything was so strange, you know?
I came here, and they introduced me to someone in the family.
There was a-- a-- a young girl who was my age, Ruth.
We're still friends.
And she was playing with Barbie dolls.
I had never had a doll.
I mean, I might have had one at the age of four, before the war.
But here I'd gone through all of this, and like, with Barbie dolls was just-- you know, it was like coming to Mars or something for me.
I was always different.
And I really want it to be the same as everybody else.
I think by high school, I probably started fitting in a little bit better.
Long Island City High School.
You know, as an aside-- this is sort of interesting.
When I was in junior high school, I made friends with this boy who-- whose mother was a widow.
And we became very good friends.
And we-- I realize now, in retrospect, that we were both sort of outsiders for whatever reason.
And this young boy became Joel Schumacher, who's the famous director.
I recently wrote to him.
I-- going through all of this memory stuff, I said, I have to write to him and say listen--
--I've been following your career--
--and I think it's great.
But the two of us were the sort of-- the outside kids, you know?
I was a very good student.
I mean, I was, like, in all the honor classes, and I was in honor English and honor history.
I was on the student court.
Yeah, I did all of the academic stuff.
Well, that was interesting, because I was so involved on the student court.
We once went to visit a-- an actual court.
And there were, like, five of us.
And the judge went around and said to everybody, well, what do you want to be?
And this person said so on and so.
I must have been the only girl, because he came to me and he said, what would you like to be?
And I said I'd like to be an attorney.
And he sort of, like, said, oh, forget it.
You know, not in those words, but more or less.
But I really-- I did not have that same-- this is-- we're talking now the '50s.
I did not think in terms of getting married.
I wanted to do something else.
I don't think so, you know?
I think, first of all, at this point, Israel had become independent, like the big push was over.
And once I was here, I really wanted to be very American.
I was sort of tired of, like, going from language to language to language.
You know, they say that math is like the last-- you-- you do math in the language that you know the most.
I never knew a language long enough.
[LAUGHTER] I remember crying because I didn't have a language that I had held on to long enough.
I was still doing math in Polish.
Then I was doing it in Hebrew.
Then I was doing it in whatever.
They were-- they would speak in Yiddish and I would speak in English.
Yes.
High school, right.
History-- still sort of, I guess, thinking in terms of going to law school.
But I-- somewhere along the line, I was told that that was not a thing that women do.
My parents kept saying, you should be a teacher, because then you could take care of the kids after school.
[LAUGHTER]
Being a teacher.
[LAUGHS]
A teacher.
OK.
And what year did you graduate from Queens College?
I actually-- I was going to be a social studies teacher.
But the first job I got, I wound up teaching a class of mentally retarded children in Greenpoint, Brooklyn.
And then the following year, I started-- I had already met Adam and was getting married.
So I subbed.
And I wound up teaching in a high school social studies-- Aviation High School.
